1.1: The Parables of Salomon, the son of David, king of Israel.
1.3: to understand the words of prudence: and to receive instruction of doctrine, justice, and judgment, and equity:
1.4: that subtility may be given to little ones, knowledge and understanding to the youngman.
1.5: the wise man hearing shall be wise: and he that understandeth shall possess governments.
1.6: He shall understand a parable, and interpretation, the words of the wise, and their dark sayings.
1.7: The fear of our Lord is the beginning of wisdom. Fools despise wisdom and doctrine.
1.8: My son, hear the discipline of thy father, and leave not the law of thy mother:
1.9: that grace may be added to thy head, and a chain of gold to thy neck.
1.10: My son, if sinners shall entice thee, condescend not to them.
1.11: If they shall say: Come with us, let us lie in wait for blood, let us hide snares against the innocent without cause:
1.12: let us swallow him alive as hell, and whole as one descending into the lake.
1.13: We shall find all precious substance, we shall fill our house with spoils.
1.14: Cast in thy lot with us, let there be one purse of us all.
1.15: My son, walk not with them, stay thy foot from their paths.
1.16: For their feet run to evil, and make haste to shed blood.
1.17: But a net is cast in vain before the eyes of them that have wings.
1.18: Themselves also lie in wait against their own blood, & practise deceits against their own souls.
1.19: So the paths of every covetous man, take violently the souls of the possessors.
1.20: Wisdom preacheth abroad, she giveth her voice in the streets.
1.21: In the head of multitudes she cryeth, in the doors of the gates of the city she uttereth her words, saying:
1.22: O children how long do you love infancy, and fools covet those things, which are hurtful to themselves, and the unwise hate knowledge?
1.23: Turn ye at my correption: behold I will utter my spirit to you, and will shew you my words.
1.24: Because I called, and you refused: I stretched out my hand, and there was none that regarded.
1.25: You have despised all my counsel, and have neglected my reprehensions.
1.26: I also will laugh in your destruction, and will scorn, when that shall come to you, which you feared.
1.27: When sudden calamity shall fall on you, and destruction as a tempest shall be at hand: when tribulation and distress shall come upon you:
1.28: Then shall they invocate me, and I will not hear: in the morning shall they arise, and shall not find me:
1.29: for that they have hated discipline, and not received the fear of our Lord,
1.30: nor consented to my counsel, & detracted from all my correption.
1.31: They shall eat therefore the fruits of their way, and shall be filled with their own counsels.
1.32: The aversion of little ones shall kill them, and the prosperity of fools shall destroy them.
1.33: But he that shall hear me, shall rest without terror, and shall enjoy aboundance, fear of evils being taken away.

2.1: My son, if thou wilt receive my words, and wilt hide my commandments with thee,
2.2: that thine ear may hear wisdom: incline thine heart to know prudence.
2.3: For if thou shalt call for wisdom, and incline thine heart to prudence:
2.4: If thou shalt seek her as money, and as treasures shalt dig her up:
2.5: then shalt thou understand the fear of our Lord, and shalt find the knowledge of God.
2.6: Because our Lord giveth wisdom: and out of his mouth prudence and knowledge.
2.7: He will keep the salvation of the righteous, & protect them that walk simply.
2.8: Keeping the paths of justice, & guarding the ways of Saints.
2.9: Then shalt thou understand justice, and judgment, and equity, and every good path.
2.10: If wisdom shall enter into thy heart, and knowledge please thy soul:
2.11: counsel shall keep thee, and prudence shall preserve thee,
2.12: that thou mayst be delivered from the evil way, and from the man that speaketh perverse things:
2.13: who leave the right way, and walk by dark ways:
2.14: who are glad when they have done evil, and rejoice in most wicked things:
2.15: whose ways are perverse, and their steps infamous.
2.16: That thou mayst be delivered from the strange woman, and from the foreigner, which mollifieth her words,
2.17: forsaketh the guide of her youth,
2.18: and hath forgotten the covenant of her God. For her house is bowed down to death, and her paths to hell.
2.19: All that go in unto her, shall not return neither shall they apprehend the paths of life.
2.20: That thou mayst walk in a good way: and mayst keep the paths of the just.
2.21: For they that are right, shall dwell in the earth, and the simple shall continue in it.
2.22: But the impious shall be destroyed from the earth: and they that do unjustly shall be taken away from it.

3.1: My son, forget not my law, and let thy heart keep my precepts.
3.2: For they shall add to thee length of days, and years of life, and peace.
3.3: Let not mercy and truth leave thee, put them about thy throat and write them in the tables of thy heart:
3.4: and thou shalt find grace; and good discipline before God and men.
3.5: Have confidence in our Lord with all thy heart and lean not upon thine own prudence.
3.6: In all thy ways think on him, and he will direct thy steps.
3.7: Be not wise in thine own conceit: fear God, and depart from evil:
3.8: for it shall be health to thy navel, and watering of thy bones.
3.9: Honour our Lord with thy substance, and give to him of the first of all thy fruits:
3.10: and thy barns shall be replenished with fullness, and thy presses shall run-over with wine.
3.11: My son, cast not away the discipline of our Lord: neither do thou faint when thou art chastised of him:
3.12: for whom our Lord loveth he chastiseth: and as a father in the son he pleaseth himself.
3.13: Blessed is the man that findeth wisdom, and floweth with prudence:
3.14: better is the purchasing thereof than merchandise of silver, and her fruit than chief and the purest gold:
3.15: she is more precious than all riches: and all things that are desired, are not able to be compared with her.
3.16: Length of days in her right hand, & in her left hand riches and glory.
3.17: Her ways are beautiful ways, and all her paths peaceable.
3.18: She is a tree of life to them that shall apprehend her: and he that shall hold her is blessed.
3.19: Our Lord by wisdom foundeth the earth, established the heavens by prudence.
3.20: By his wisdom the depths have broken forth, and the clouds wax thick with dew.
3.21: My son, let not these things depart from thine eyes: keep the law & counsel:
3.22: and there shall be life to thy soul, and grace to thy jaws.
3.23: Then shalt thou walk confidently in thy way, and thy foot shall not stumble:
3.24: if thou sleep, thou shalt not fear: thou shalt rest, and thy sleep shall be sweet.
3.25: Dread not at sudden terror, and the power of the impious falling upon thee.
3.26: For our Lord will be at thy side, and will keep thy foot that thou be not taken.
3.27: Do not prohibit him to do good, that is able: if thou be able, thyself also do good.
3.28: Say not to thy friend: go, and return; and tomorrow I will give to thee: whereas thou mayst give forthwith.
3.29: Practise not evil against thy friend, when he hath affiance in thee.
3.30: Contend not against man without cause, whereas he hath done thee no evil.
3.31: Do not envy an unjust man, nor imitate his ways:
3.32: because every deluder is an abomination of our Lord, and his communication is with the simple.
3.33: There is poverty from our Lord in the house of the impious: but the habitations of the just shall be blessed.
3.34: He shall delude the deluders, and to the mild he will give grace.
3.35: The wise shall possess glory: the exaltation of fools ignominy.

4.1: Children hear ye the father's discipline, and attend that you may know prudence.
4.2: I will give you a good gift, forsake not my law.
4.3: For I also was the son of my father, tender and as the only-begotten in my mother's sight:
4.4: and he taught me, & said: Let thy heart receive my words, keep my precepts, and thou shalt live.
4.5: Possess wisdom, possess prudence: forget not, neither decline from the words of my mouth.
4.6: Leave her not, and she will keep thee: love her, and she will preserve thee.
4.7: The beginning of wisdom, possess wisdom, and in all thy possession purchase prudence:
4.8: take her quickly, and she will exalt thee: thou shalt be glorified of her, when thou shalt embrace her.
4.9: She will give to thy head increase of graces, and with a noble crown she will protect thee.
4.10: Hear my son, and receive my words, that years of life may be multiplied to thee.
4.11: The way of wisdom I will shew to thee, I will lead thee by the paths of equity.
4.12: Which when thou shalt have entered, thy steps shall not be straitened, and running thou shalt not have a stumbling block.
4.13: Hold discipline, leave it not: keep it, because the same is thy life.
4.14: Be not delighted in the paths of the impious, neither let the way of the evil please thee.
4.15: Fly from it, neither pass thou by it: go aside, and forsake it.
4.16: For they sleep not unless they have done ill: and they take no sleep unless they supplant.
4.17: They eat the bread of impiety, and drink the wine of iniquity.
4.18: But the path of the just, as shining light, proceedeth even to perfect day.
4.19: The way of the impious is darksome: they know not where they fall.
4.20: My son hear my words, and incline thine ear to my sayings.
4.21: Let them not depart from thine eyes, keep them in the midst of thy heart.
4.22: For they are life to those that find them, and health to all flesh.
4.23: With all guard keep thy heart, because life proceedeth from it.
4.24: Remove from thee a froward mouth, and let detracting lips be far from thee.
4.25: Let thine eyes see right things, & let thine eye-lids go before thy steps.
4.26: Direct the path to thy feet, and all thy ways shall be established.
4.27: Decline not to the right hand, nor to the left: turn away thy foot from evil. For our Lord knoweth the ways that are on the right hand: but those are perverse, which are on the left hand. But he will make thy courses right, and thy ways he will bring forward in peace.

5.1: My son, attend to my wisdom, and to my prudence incline thine ear,
5.2: that thou mayst keep cogitations, and thy lips preserve discipline.
5.3: Attend not to the deceit of a woman: for the lips of an harlot are as a honey comb distilling, and her throat neater than oil.
5.4: But her later ends are bitter as wormwood, and sharp as a two edged sword.
5.5: Her feet go down into death, and her steps penetrate unto hell.
5.6: They walk not by the path of life, her steps are wandering, and unsearchable.
5.7: Now therefore my son hear me, and depart not from the words of my mouth.
5.8: Make thy way far from her, and approach not to the doors of her house.
5.9: Give not thy honour to strangers, and thy years to the cruel.
5.10: Lest perhaps strangers be filled with thy strength, and thy labours be in another man's house,
5.11: and thou mourn in the end, when thou shalt have spent thy flesh and thy body, and say:
5.12: Why have I detested discipline, and my heart consented not to reprehensions,
5.13: nor I heard the voice of them that taught me, and have not inclined mine ear to masters?
5.14: I have almost been in all evil, in the midst of the Church and of the synagogue.
5.15: Drink water of thine own cistern, and the streams of thy well:
5.16: Let thy fountains be derived abroad, and in the streets divide thy waters,
5.17: Have them alone, neither let strangers be partakers with thee.
5.18: Let thy vein be blessed, and rejoice with the woman of thy youth:
5.19: a hind most dear, and a most grateful fawn: let her breasts inebriate thee at all time, in her love be thou delighted continually.
5.20: Why art thou seduced my son of a strange woman, and art cherished in the bosom of another?
5.21: Our Lord beholdeth the ways of man, and considereth all his steps.
5.22: His own iniquities take the impious, and he is fast bound with the ropes of his sins.
5.23: He shall die because he hath not had discipline, and in the multitude of his folly he shall be deceived.

6.1: My son if thou shalt be surety for thy friend, and hast made fast thy hand to a stranger,
6.2: thou art entrapped with the words of thy mouth, & caught with thine own words.
6.3: Do therefore my son that which I say, and deliver thyself, because thou art fallen into the hand of thy neighbour. Run diverse ways, make haste, raise thy friend.
6.4: Give not sleep to thine eyes, neither let thine eye-lids slumber.
6.5: Deliver thyself as a little doe from the hand, and as a bird from the hand of the fowler.
6.6: Go to the emmot, o sluggard, and consider her ways, and learn wisdom.
6.7: Who whereas she hath no guide, nor master, nor captain,
6.8: prepareth meat for herself in the summer, and gathereth in the harvest for to eat.
6.9: How long wilt thou sleep, o sluggard? When wilt thou rise out of thy sleep?
6.10: Thou shalt sleep a little, a little shalt thou slumber, a little shalt thou join thy hands to sleep:
6.11: and penury shall come to thee, as a wayfaring man, and poverty as a man armed. But if thou be not sluggish, thy harvest shall come as a fountain, and penury shall fly far from thee.
6.12: Aman that is an Apostata, a man unprofitable, goeth with perverse mouth,
6.13: winketh with the eyes, treadeth with the foot, speaketh with the finger,
6.14: with wicked heart he deviseth evil, and at all time he soweth brawls.
6.15: To him his destruction shall come forthwith, and he shall suddenly be destroyed, neither shall he have remedy any more.
6.16: Six things there are which our Lord hateth, and the seventh his soul detesteth:
6.17: Lofty eyes, a lying tongue, hands that shed innocent blood,
6.18: a heart that deviseth most wicked devices, feet swift to run into evil,
6.19: a deceitful witness that uttereth lies, and him that among brethren soweth discords.
6.20: My son keep the precepts of thy father, and leave not the law of thy mother.
6.21: Bind them in thy heart continually, and put them about thy throat.
6.22: When thou shalt walk, let them go with thee: when thou shalt sleep, let them keep thee, and awaking talk with them.
6.23: Because the commandment is a lamp, and the law a light, and the way of life the increpation of discipline:
6.24: that they may keep thee from the evil woman, and from the fair spoken tongue of the stranger.
6.25: Let not thy heart covet her beauty, be not caught with her becks:
6.26: for the price of an harlot is scarce worth one loaf: but a woman catcheth the precious soul of man.
6.27: Can a man hide fire in his bosom, that his garments burn not?
6.28: Or walk upon hot coals, that his soles be not burnt?
6.29: so he that goeth in unto his neighbour's wife, shall not be clean when he shall touch her.
6.30: It is no great fault, when a man shall have stolen: for he stealeth to fill his hungry soul:
6.31: also being taken he shall restore sevenfold, and shall give up all the substance of his house.
6.32: But he that is an adulterer, for penury of heart shall destroy his own soul:
6.33: shame and ignominy he gathereth to himself, & his reproach shall not be blotted out.
6.34: Because the zeal and fury of the husband will not spare in the day of revenge,
6.35: neither will he yield to any man's prayers, neither will he take for redemption very many gifts.

7.1: My son keep my words, and my precepts hide with thee. Son,
7.2: keep my commandments, and thou shalt live: and my law as the apple of thine eye:
7.3: bind it on thy fingers, write it in the tables of thy heart.
7.4: Say to wisdom, thou art my sister: & call prudence thy friend,
7.5: that she may keep thee from the strange woman, and from the foreigner which maketh her words sweet.
7.6: For out of the window of my house I looked out through the lattice,
7.7: and I see little ones, I behold a foolish youngman,
7.8: which passeth through the streets by the corner, and goeth nigh the way of her house,
7.9: in the dark the day being toward evening, in the darkness of the night, and dimness.
7.10: And behold the woman meeteth him in harlot's attire, prepared to deceive souls: babbling and wandering,
7.11: impatient of rest, nor able to consist in the house on her feet,
7.12: now abroad, now in the streets, now lying in wait near the corners.
7.13: And taking the youngman she kisseth him, and with malapert countenance speaketh fair, saying:
7.14: I vowed victims for welfare, this day I have payed my vows.
7.15: Therefore I am come forth to meet thee, desirous to see, and I have found thee.
7.16: I have woven my bed with cords, I have adorned it with tapestry pictured out of Ægypt.
7.17: I have sprinkled my bed with myrrh, aloes, and cinnamon.
7.18: Come let us be inebriated with breasts, and let us enjoy desired embracings, till the day appear.
7.19: For my husband is not at home, he is gone a very long journey.
7.20: he carried with him a bag of money: in the day of the full moon he will return to his house.
7.21: She entangled him with many words, and with flattery of lips drew him.
7.22: Immediately he followeth her as an oxe led to be a victim, & as a lamb playing the wanton, and not knowing that he is drawn as a fool to bonds,
7.23: till the arrow pierce his liver: as if a bird should make haste to the snare, and knoweth not that his life is in danger.
7.24: Now therefore my son, hear me, and attend to the words of my mouth.
7.25: Let not thy mind be drawn away in her ways: neither be thou deceived with her paths.
7.26: For she hath cast down many wounded, and all the most strong are slain by her.
7.27: Her house the ways of hell, penetrating to the inner parts of the death.

8.1: Doth not wisdom cry, and prudence give her voice?
8.2: Standing in the high & lofty tops over the way, in the midst of the paths,
8.3: beside the gates of the city in the very doors she speaketh, saying:
8.4: O men, to you I cry, and my voice is to the children of men.
8.5: O little ones understand subtilty, and ye unwise mark.
8.6: Hear ye, because I will speak of great things: and my lips shall be opened to preach right things.
8.7: My throat shall meditate truth, and my lips shall detest the impious.
8.8: All my words are just, there is no wicked, nor perverse thing in them.
8.9: They are right to them that understand, and just to them that find knowledge.
8.10: Receive ye discipline, & not money: choose doctrine rather than gold.
8.11: For wisdom is better than all most precious riches: and whatsoever is to be desired cannot be compared to it.
8.12: I wisdom dwell in counsel, and am present in learned cogitations.
8.13: The fear of our Lord hateth evil: arrogancy, and pride, and wicked way, and a double tongued mouth I do detest.
8.14: Mine is counsel & equity, prudence is mine, strength is mine.
8.15: By me kings do reign, and the makers of laws decree just things.
8.16: By me Princes rule, and the mighty decree justice.
8.17: I love them that love me: and they that watch toward me shall find me.
8.18: With me are riches, and glory, glorious riches, and justice.
8.19: For my fruit is better than gold and precious stone, and my blossoms than chosen silver.
8.20: I walk in the ways of justice, in the midst of the paths of judgment,
8.21: that I may enrich them that love me, and may replenish their treasures.
8.22: Our Lord possessed me in the beginning of his ways, before he had made any thing from the beginning.
8.23: From eternity I was ordained & of old before the earth was made.
8.24: The depths were not as yet, and I was now conceived, neither had the fountains of waters as yet gushed forth:
8.25: the mountains with heavy hugeness stood not as yet: before the little hills I was brought forth:
8.26: he had not yet made the earth, and the rivers, and the poles of the round world.
8.27: When he prepared the heavens, I was present: when with a certain law and circuit he compassed the depths:
8.28: When he established the firmament above, & poised the fountains of waters:
8.29: when he compassed the sea with her limits, and set a law to the waters that they should not pass their bounds: when he hanged the foundations of the earth,
8.30: I was with him framing all things: and was delighted every day, playing before him at all time,
8.31: playing in the world: & my delights to be with the children of men.
8.32: Now therefore children hear me: Blessed are they that keep my ways.
8.33: Hear ye discipline, and be wise, and reject it not.
8.34: Blessed is the man that heareth me, & that watcheth at my doors daily, & waiteth at the posts of my door.
8.35: He that shall find me shall find life, and shall draw salvation of our Lord:
8.36: but he that shall sin against me, shall hurt his own soul. All that hate me, love death.

9.1: Wisdom hath built herself an house, she hath cut out seven pillars.
9.2: She hath immolated her victims, mingled her wine, and set forth her table.
9.3: She hath sent her hand-maids to call to the tower, and to the walls of the city:
9.4: If any be a little one, let him come to me. And to the unwise she spake:
9.5: Come, eat ye my bread, & drink the wine which I have mingled for you.
9.6: Leave infancy, and live, and walk by the ways of prudence.
9.7: He that teacheth a scorner doth injury to himself; and he that rebuketh the impious, purchaseth a blot to himself.
9.8: Rebuke not the scorner lest he hate thee. Rebuke a wise man, and he will love thee.
9.9: Give occasion to a wise man, and wisdom shall be added to him. Teach the just, and he shall make haste to take it.
9.10: The beginning of wisdom, the fear of our Lord: and the knowledge of the holy, prudence.
9.11: For by me shall thy days be multiplied, and years of life shall be added to thee.
9.12: If thou be wise, to thyself thou shalt be: and if a scorner, thou alone shalt bear the evil.
9.13: Afoolish woman and clamorous, and full of allurements, and knowing nothing at all,
9.14: sat in the doors of her house upon a seat in a high place of the city,
9.15: to call them that pass by the way, and go on their journey:
9.16: he that is a little one, let him turn to me. And to the fool she spake:
9.17: Stolen waters are sweeter, and hidden bread more pleasant.
9.18: And he was ignorant that giants are there, and her guests in the depths of hell.

10.1: Awise son maketh the father glad: but a foolish son is the sorrow of his mother.
10.2: The treasures of impiety shall profit nothing: but justice shall deliver from death.
10.3: Our Lord will not afflict with famine the soul of the just, and the deceitful practices of the impious he will overthrow.
10.4: The slothful hand hath wrought poverty: the hand of the strong getteth riches. Who so trusteth to lies feedeth the winds: and the self-same man followeth the flying birds.
10.5: He that gathereth in the harvest, is a wise son: but he that snorteth in summer, is the son of confusion.
10.6: The blessing of our Lord is upon the head of the just: but iniquity covereth the mouth of the impious.
10.7: The memory of the just is with praises: and the name of the impious shall rot.
10.8: The wise of heart shall receive precepts: a fool is beaten with lips.
10.9: He that walketh plainly, walketh confidently: but he that depraveth his ways, shall be manifest.
10.10: He that winketh with the eye, shall give sorrow: and the fool in lips shall be beaten.
10.11: Avein of life, the mouth of the just: and the mouth of the impious covereth iniquity.
10.12: Hatred raiseth brawls: and charity covereth all sins.
10.13: In the lips of the wise wisdom is found: and a rod on his back that lacketh wit.
10.14: Wise men hide knowledge: but the mouth of the fool is next to confusion.
10.15: The substance of a rich man, is a city of his strength: the fear of the poor their poverty.
10.16: The work of the just unto life: but the fruit of the impious unto sin.
10.17: The way of life, to him that keepeth discipline: but he that forsaketh reprehensions, erreth.
10.18: Lying lips hide hatred: he that uttereth contumely is unwise.
10.19: In much talk there shall not want sin: but he that moderateth his lips is most wise.
10.20: The tongue of the just, is chosen silver: but the heart of the impious is nothing worth.
10.21: The lips of the just teach very many: but they that are untaught, shall die in the penury of wit.
10.22: The blessing of our Lord maketh men rich: neither shall affliction be joined to them.
10.23: Afool worketh mischief as it were by laughter: but wisdom is prudence to a man.
10.24: That which the impious feareth, shall come upon him: to the just their desire shall be given.
10.25: As a tempest passing the impious shall not be: but the just as an everlasting foundation.
10.26: As vinegar to the teeth, and smoke to the eyes, so a sluggard to them that sent him.
10.27: The fear of our Lord shall add days: and the years of the impious shall be shortened.
10.28: The expectation of the just is joy: but the hope of the impious shall perish.
10.29: The strength of the simple the way of our Lord: and fear is to them that work evil.
10.30: The just for ever shall not be moved: but the impious shall not dwell on the earth.
10.31: The mouth of the just shall bring forth wisdom: the tongue of the froward shall perish.
10.32: The lips of the just consider grateful things: and the mouth of the impious perverse things.

11.1: Adeceitful balance, is abomination before God: and an equal weight is his will.
11.2: Where pride shall be, there shall be contumely also: but where humility, there also wisdom.
11.3: The simplicity of the just shall direct them: and the supplanting of the perverse shall waste them.
11.4: Riches shall not profit in the day of revenge: but justice shall deliver from death.
11.5: The justice of the simple shall direct his way: and the impious shall fall in his impiety.
11.6: The justice of the righteous shall deliver them: and the unjust shall be caught in their deceitful practices.
11.7: The impious man being dead, there shall be no hope any more: and the expectation of the careful shall perish.
11.8: The just is delivered from distress: and the impious shall be given for him.
11.9: The dissembler with his mouth deceiveth his friend: but the just shall be delivered by knowledge.
11.10: In the good things of the just the city shall rejoice; and in the destruction of the impious there shall be praise.
11.11: With the benediction of the just the city shall be exalted: and by the mouth of the impious it shall be subverted.
11.12: He that despiseth his friend, lacketh heart: but the wise man will hold his peace.
11.13: He that walketh fraudulently, revealeth secrets: but he that is faithful, concealeth the thing committed of his friend.
11.14: Where there is no governor, the people shall fall: but there is health where is much counsel.
11.15: He shall be afflicted with evil, that is surety for a stranger: but he that is ware of the snares, shall be secure.
11.16: Agracious woman shall find glory: and the strong shall have riches.
11.17: A merciful man doth good to his kindred also. Benefacit animæ suæ vir misericors; qui autem crudelis est, etiam propinquos abjicit.
11.18: The impious maketh an unstable work: but to him that soweth justice, is a faithful reward.
11.19: Clemency prepareth life: & the pursuing of evil things death.
11.20: Aperverse heart is abominable to our Lord: and his will is in them that walk simply.
11.21: Hand in hand the evil man shall not be innocent: but the seed of the just shall be saved.
11.22: Aring of gold in a swine's snout, a fair woman & a fool.
11.23: The desire of the just is all good: the expectation of the impious fury.
11.24: Some divide their own goods, and are made richer: others take violently not their own, and are always in poverty.
11.25: The soul which blesseth, shall be made fat: and he that inebriateth himself shall also be inebriated.
11.26: He that hideth corn, shall be cursed among the peoples: but blessing upon the head of them that sell.
11.27: Well riseth he early who seeketh good things: but he that is a searcher after evil things, shall be oppressed of them.
11.28: He that trusteth in his riches shall fall: but the just shall spring as a green leaf.
11.29: He that troubleth his house, shall possess the winds: and he that is a fool shall serve the wise.
11.30: The fruit of the just man a tree of life: and he that gaineth souls, is wise.
11.31: If the just man receive in the earth, how much more the impious and sinner?

12.1: He that loveth discipline, loveth knowledge: but he that hateth reprehensions is unwise.
12.2: He that is good, shall draw grace from our Lord: but he that trusteth in his own cogitations, doth impiously.
12.3: Man shall not be strengthened by impiety: and the root of the just shall not be moved.
12.4: Adiligent woman is a crown to her husband: and putrefaction in his bones, she that doth things worthy of confusion.
12.5: The cogitations of the just are judgments: & the counsels of the impious are fraudulent.
12.6: The words of the impious lie in wait for blood: the mouth of the just shall deliver them.
12.7: Turn the impious, and they shall not be: but the house of the just shall be permanent.
12.8: Aman shall be known by his doctrine: but he that is vain and foolish, shall lie open to contempt.
12.9: Better is the poor and sufficient to himself, than he that is glorious and wanteth bread.
12.10: The just knoweth the lives of his beasts: but the bowels of the impious are cruel.
12.11: He that tilleth his land, shall be filled with breads: but he that pursueth idleness is a very fool. He that is delighted in much quaffing of wine, leaveth contumely in his munitions.
12.12: The desire of the impious is the muniment of the most wicked: but the root of the just shall prosper.
12.13: For the sins of the lips' ruin approacheth to the evil man: but the just escapeth out of distress.
12.14: Of the fruit of his own mouth shall every man be replenished with good things, and according to the works of his hands it shall be repayed him.
12.15: The way of a fool is right in his eyes: but he that is wise heareth counsels.
12.16: Afool by & by sheweth his anger: but he that dissembleth injuries is wise.
12.17: He that speaketh that which he knoweth, is an utterer of justice: but he that lieth, is a fraudulent witness.
12.18: There is that promiseth, and is pricked as it were with the sword of conscience: but the tongue of the wise is health.
12.19: The lip of truth shall be stable for ever: but he that is an hasty witness, frameth a tongue of lying.
12.20: Guile is in the heart of them that think evil things: but joy followeth them that give counsels of peace.
12.21: It shall not make the just sorry what soever shall fall to him: but the impious shall be replenished with evil.
12.22: Lying lips are an abomination to our Lord: but they that do faithfully please him.
12.23: Acircumspect man concealeth knowledge: and the heart of the unwise provoketh folly.
12.24: The hand of the strong shall rule, but that which is slothful, shall serve under tributes.
12.25: Pensiveness in the heart of a man shall humble him, & with a good word he shall be made glad.
12.26: He that neglecteth damage for a friend, is just: but the way of the impious shall deceive them.
12.27: The fraudulent man shall not find gain: and the substance of a man shall be the price of gold.
12.28: In the path of justice, life: but the by-way leadeth to death.

13.1: Awise son is the doctrine of the father: but he that is a scorner, heareth not when he is rebuked.
13.2: Of the fruit of his own mouth man shall be filled with good things: but the soul of the prevaricators is wicked.
13.3: He that keepeth his mouth, keepeth his soul: but he that is unadvised to speak shall feel evils.
13.4: The sluggard will and will not: but the soul of them that work, shall be made fat.
13.5: The just shall detest a lying word: but the impious confoundeth, and shall be confounded.
13.6: Justice keepeth the way of the innocent: but impiety supplanteth the sinner.
13.7: There is one as it were with riches whereas he hath nothing: and there is as it were poor, whereas he is in much riches.
13.8: The redemption of a man's life, his riches: but he that is poor, beareth not reprehension.
13.9: The light of the just maketh glad: but the candle of the impious shall be extinguished.
13.10: Among the proud there are always brawls: but they that do all things with counsel, are ruled by wisdom.
13.11: Substance hastened shall be diminished: but that which by little and little is gathered with the hand, shall be multiplied.
13.12: Hope that is deferred afflicteth the soul: a tree of life the desire of ckoning.
13.13: Who so detracteth from any thing, he bindeth himself for the time to come: but he that feareth the precept, shall converse in peace. Guileful souls err in sins: the just are merciful & have pity.
13.14: The law of a wise man a fountain of life, that he may decline from the ruin of death.
13.15: Good doctrine shall give grace: in the way of contemners a whirlpool.
13.16: The subtle man doth all things with counsel: but he that is a fool openeth folly.
13.17: The messenger of the impious shall fall into evil: but a faithful legate, is health.
13.18: Poverty, and ignominy, to him that forsaketh discipline: but he that yieldeth to him that rebuketh, shall be glorified.
13.19: Desire if it be accomplished, delighteth the soul: fools detest them that fly evil things.
13.20: He that walketh with the wise, shall be wise: a friend of fools shall be made like.
13.21: Evil pursueth sinners, and to the just good things shall be repayed.
13.22: The good man leaveth heirs, sons, and nephews: and the substance of the sinner is kept for the just.
13.23: Much meat in the tilled grounds of the fathers: and to others they are gathered without judgment.
13.24: He that spareth the rod, hateth his child: but he that loveth him doth instantly nurture him.
13.25: The just eateth and filleth his soul: but the belly of the impious unsatiable.

14.1: Awise woman buildeth her house: the unwise will with her hands destroy that also which is built.
14.2: He that walketh in the right way, & feareth God, is despised of him, that goeth an infamous way.
14.3: In the mouth of a fool the rod of pride: but the lips of the wise keep them.
14.4: Where oxen are not, the stall is empty: but where much corn is, there is the ox's strength manifest.
14.5: Afaithful witness will not lie: but a deceitful witness uttereth a lie.
14.6: Ascorner seeketh wisdom and findeth it not: the doctrine of the prudent is easy.
14.7: Go against a foolish man, and he knoweth not the lips of prudence.
14.8: The wisdom of a discreet man is to understand his way: and the imprudence of fools erreth.
14.9: Afool will laugh at sin, & among the just grace shall abide.
14.10: The heart that knoweth the bitterness of his soul, in his joy shall not the stranger be mingled.
14.11: The house of the impious shall be rased: the tabernacles of the just shall spring.
14.12: There is a way, which seemeth to a man just: but the later ends thereof lead to death.
14.13: Laughter shall be mingled with sorrow, and mourning occupieth the later ends of joy.
14.14: Afool shall be replenished with his ways, and the good man shall be above him.
14.15: The innocent believeth every word: the discreet man considereth his steps.
14.16: Awise man feareth and declineth from evil: the fool leapeth over and is confident.
14.17: The impatient man shall work folly: and the subtle man is odious.
14.18: The childish man shall possess folly, and the prudent shall expect knowledge.
14.19: The evil shall lie down before the good, and the impious before the gates of the just.
14.20: The poor shall be odious even to his neighbour: but the friends of the rich be many.
14.21: He that despiseth his neighbour, sinneth: but he that hath pity on the poor, shall be blessed.
14.22: They err that work evil: mercy and truth prepare good things.
14.23: In every work there shall be aboundance: but where many words are, there is oftentimes poverty.
14.24: The crown of the wise, their riches: the folly of fools, imprudence.
14.25: Afaithful witness delivereth souls: and the dissembler uttereth lies.
14.26: In the fear of our Lord is confidence of strength, and to his children there shall be hope.
14.27: The fear of our Lord a fountain of life, that he may decline from the ruin of death.
14.28: In the multitude of people the dignity of the king: and in fewness of people the ignominy of the Prince.
14.29: He that is patient, is governed with much wisdom: but he that is impatient, exalteth his folly.
14.30: Health of heart, the life of the flesh: envy, the putrefaction of the bones.
14.31: He that doth calumniate the needy, upbraideth his maker: but he honoureth him, that hath pity on the poor.
14.32: The impious shall be expelled in his malice: but the just hopeth in his death.
14.33: In the heart of the prudent resteth wisdom, & it shall instruct all the unlearned.
14.34: Justice advanceth a nation: but sin maketh peoples miserable.
14.35: Aservant that understandeth is acceptable to the king: he that is unprofitable shall sustain his anger.

15.1: Asoft answer breaketh anger: and a hard word raiseth up fury.
15.2: The tongue of the wise adorneth knowledge: the mouth of fools boileth forth folly.
15.3: In every place the eyes of our Lord behold the good and the evil.
15.4: Apeaceable tongue is a tree of life: but that which is immoderate, shall break the spirit.
15.5: Afool scorneth the discipline of his father: but he that regardeth reprehensions, shall become more prudent. In abundant justice there is greater force: but the cogitations of the impious shall be rooted out.
15.6: The house of the just is very much strength: and in the fruits of the impious is perturbation.
15.7: The lips of the wise shall sow knowledge: the heart of fools shall be unlike.
15.8: The victims of the impious are abominable to our Lord: the vows of the just are acceptable.
15.9: The way of the impious is abomination to our Lord: he that followeth justice is beloved of him.
15.10: The doctrine is evil of them that forsake the way of life: he that hateth reprehensions shall die.
15.11: Hell and perdition are before our Lord: how much more the hearts of the children of men?
15.12: The pestilent man loveth not him that rebuketh him: nor goeth to the wise.
15.13: Aglad heart cheereth the face: in pensiveness of mind the spirit is cast down.
15.14: The heart of the wise seeketh doctrine: and the mouth of fools is fed with unskillfulness.
15.15: All the days of the poor are evil: a secure mind is as it were a continual feast.
15.16: Better is a little with the fear of our Lord, than great treasures and unsatiable.
15.17: It is better to be called to herbs with charity: than to a fatted calf with hatred.
15.18: An angry man stirreth brawls: he that is patient appeaseth those that are raised.
15.19: The way of the slothful is as an hedge of thorns: the way of the just is without offence.
15.20: Awise son maketh the father joyful: and the foolish man despiseth his mother.
15.21: Folly is joy to a fool: and the wise man directeth his steps.
15.22: Cogitations are dissipated where there is no counsel: but where many counsellers are, they are confirmed.
15.23: Aman rejoiceth in the sentence of his mouth: and a word in due time is best.
15.24: The path of life above the learned, that he may decline from the lowest hell.
15.25: Our Lord will destroy the house of the proud: and will make sure the borders of the widow.
15.26: Evil cogitations are an abomination to our Lord: and pure speech most beautiful shall be confirmed of him.
15.27: He that pursueth avarice disturbeth his house: but he that hateth gifts shall live. By mercy and faith sins are purged: and by the fear of our Lord every one declineth from evil.
15.28: The mind of the just meditateth obedience: the mouth of the impious redoundeth with evils.
15.29: Our Lord is far from the impious: and he will hear the prayers of the just.
15.30: The light of the eyes rejoiceth the soul: a good name fatteth the bones.
15.31: The ear that heareth the reprehensions of life, shall abide in the midst of the wise.
15.32: He that rejecteth discipline, despiseth his soul: but he that yieldeth to reprehensions, is a possessor of the heart.
15.33: The fear of our Lord, the discipline of wisdom: and humility goeth before glory.

16.1: It pertaineth to man to prepare the heart: and to our Lord to govern the tongue.
16.2: All the ways of man are open to his eyes: our Lord is the weigher of spirits.
16.3: Reveal thy works to our Lord: and thy cogitations shall be directed.
16.4: Our Lord hath wrought all things for himself: the impious also to the evil day.
16.5: Every arrogant man is an abomination to our Lord: although hand shall be to hand he is not innocent. The beginning of a good way, is to do justice: and it is more acceptable with God, than to immolate hosts.
16.6: By mercy and truth iniquity is redeemed: and in the fear of our Lord evil is avoided.
16.7: When the ways of man shall please our Lord, he will convert also his enemies to peace.
16.8: Better is a little with justice, than much fruit with iniquity.
16.9: The heart of man disposeth his way: but it pertaineth to our Lord to direct his progress.
16.10: Divination is in the lips of the king, his mouth shall not err in judgment.
16.11: Weight and balance are judgments of our Lord: and his work all the stones of the bag.
16.12: They are abominable to the king that do impiously: because the throne is established by justice.
16.13: The will of kings are just lips: he that speaketh right things shall be beloved.
16.14: The king's indignation, messengers of death: and the wise man will pacify it.
16.15: In the cheerfulness of the king's countenance is life: and his clemency is as the later shower.
16.16: Possess wisdom, because it is better than gold: and get prudence, because it is more precious than silver.
16.17: The path of the just avoideth evils: the keeper of his soul keepeth his way.
16.18: Pride goeth before destruction, and before ruin the spirit shall be exalted.
16.19: It is better to be humbled with the meek, than to divide spoils with the proud.
16.20: The learned in word shall find good things: and he that hopeth in our Lord, is blessed.
16.21: He that is wise in heart, shall be called prudent: and he that is sweet in speech shall find greater things.
16.22: Afountain of life the learning of him that possesseth it: the doctrine of fools foolishness.
16.23: The heart of the wise shall instruct his mouth: and shall add grace to his lips.
16.24: Well set words are a honey comb: sweetness of the soul the health of the bones.
16.25: There is a way that seemeth to a man right: and the later ends thereof lead to death.
16.26: The soul of him that laboureth doth labour to himself, because his mouth hath compelled him.
16.27: The impious man diggeth evil, and in his lips fire burneth.
16.28: Aperverse man raiseth contentions: and one full of words separateth Princes.
16.29: An unjust man allureth his friend: and leadeth him by a way not good.
16.30: He that with astonished eyes thinketh wicked things, biting his lips bringeth evil to pass.
16.31: Acrown of dignity old age, which shall be found in the ways of justice.
16.32: Better is the patient than a strong man: and he that ruleth his mind, than the overthrower of cities.
16.33: Lots are cast into the bosom, but they are ordered of our Lord.

17.1: Better is a dry morsel with joy, than a house full of victims with brawling.
17.2: Awise servant shall rule over foolish children, and divide inheritance among brethren.
17.3: As silver is tried by fire, and gold in the furnace: so our Lord proveth the hearts.
17.4: The evil man obeyeth an unjust tongue: and the deceitful obeyeth lying lips.
17.5: He that despiseth the poor, upbraideth his maker: and he that rejoiceth at another man's ruin, shall not be unpunished.
17.6: The crown of old men the children's children: and the glory of children their fathers.
17.7: Eloquent words become not a fool, nor lying lips a Prince.
17.8: Amost grateful pearl, the expectation of him that expecteth: whither soever he turneth himself, he understandeth wisely.
17.9: He that concealeth offence, seeketh friendships: he that in other word repeateth it, separateth the confederate.
17.10: Reprehension doth more profit with a wiseman, than an hundred stripes with a fool.
17.11: An evil man always seeketh brawls: but a cruel angel shall be sent against him.
17.12: It is better to meet a bear when her young are taken away, than a fool trusting to himself in his own folly.
17.13: He that rendereth evil things for good, evil shall not depart from his house.
17.14: He that letteth water go, is the head of brawls: & before he suffer contumely, he forsaketh judgment.
17.15: He that justifieth the impious, and he that condemneth the just, both are abominable before God.
17.16: What doth it profit a fool to have riches, whereas he cannot buy wisdom? He that maketh his house high, seeketh ruin: and he that refuseth to learn, shall fall into evils.
17.17: He loveth at all time that is a friend: and a brother is proved in distresses.
17.18: Afoolish man will clap the hands, when he is surety for his friend.
17.19: He that meditateth discords, loveth brawls: and he that exalteth the door, seeketh ruin.
17.20: He that is of a perverse heart, shall not find good: and he that turneth his tongue, shall fall into evil.
17.21: Afool is born to his own ignominy: but neither shall the father rejoice in a fool.
17.22: Ajoyful mind maketh a flourishing age, a sorrowful spirit dryeth up the bones.
17.23: The impious receiveth gifts out of the bosom, that he may pervert the paths of judgment.
17.24: In the face of the prudent wisdom shineth: the eyes of fools are in the ends of the earth.
17.25: Afoolish son is the anger of the father: and the sorrow of the mother that bare him.
17.26: It is not good to do hurt to the just: nor to strike the Prince, which judgeth right.
17.27: He that moderateth his words, is learned and prudent: and the learned man is of a precious spirit.
17.28: The fool also if he hold his peace, shall be reputed wise: and if he close his lips, a man of understanding.

18.1: He seeketh occasions that will depart from a friend: he shall ever be subject to reproach.
18.2: Afool receiveth not the words of prudence: unless thou say those things which are in his heart.
18.3: The impious when he shall come into the depth of sins, contemneth: but ignominy and reproach follow him.
18.4: Deep water words from the mouth of a man: and a stream overflowing the fountain of wisdom.
18.5: To accept the person of the impious in judgment is not good, that thou decline from the truth of judgment.
18.6: The lips of a fool mingle him with strife: and his mouth provoketh brawls.
18.7: The mouth of a fool is his destruction: and his lips are the ruin of his soul.
18.8: The words of the double tongued, as it were simple: and the same come even to the inner part of the belly. Fear casteth down the slothful: and the souls of the effeminate shall be hungry.
18.9: He that is soft and dissolute in his work, is the brother of him that destroyeth his own works.
18.10: Amost strong tower, the name of our Lord: the just runneth to it, and shall be exalted.
18.11: The substance of the rich man a city of his strength: and as a strong wall compassing him about.
18.12: Before he be broken, the heart of a man is exalted: and before he be glorified, it is humbled.
18.13: He that answereth before he hear, sheweth himself to be a fool, and worthy of confusion.
18.14: The spirit of a man upholdeth his imbecility: but a spirit that is easy to be angry who can sustain?
18.15: Awise heart shall possess knowledge: and the ear of the wise seeketh doctrine.
18.16: The gift of a man enlargeth his way, & maketh him room before Princes.
18.17: The just is first accuser of himself: his friend cometh, and shall search him.
18.18: Lot suppresseth contradictions, and between the mighty also it determineth.
18.19: Brother that is holpen of brother, is a strong city: and judgments are as the bars of cities.
18.20: Of the fruit of man's mouth his belly shall be filled: and the offsprings of his lips shall fill him.
18.21: Death and life in the hand of the tongue: they that love it, shall eat the fruits thereof.
18.22: He that hath found a good wife, hath found a good thing, and hath received a pleasure of our Lord.
18.23: The poor speaketh with supplications, and the rich will speak roughly.
18.24: Aman amiable to society, shall be more friendly than a brother.

19.1: Better is a poor man, that walketh in his simplicity, than a rich writhing his lips, and unwise.
19.2: Where is no knowledge of the soul, is not good: and he that is hasty with his feet shall stumble.
19.3: The folly of a man supplanteth his steps: and he boileth in his mind against God.
19.4: Riches add many friends: but from the poor they also which he had are separated.
19.5: Afalse witness shall not be unpunished: & he that speaketh lies, shall not escape.
19.6: Many worship the person of the mighty, and are friends of him that giveth gifts.
19.7: The brethren of the poor man hate him: moreover also his friends have departed far from him.
19.8: He that pursueth words only, shall have nothing: but he that is possessor of the mind, loveth his soul, and the keeper of prudence shall find good things.
19.9: Afalse witness shall not be unpunished: and he that speaketh lies shall perish.
19.10: Delicacies become not a fool: nor a servant to rule over Princes.
19.11: The doctrine of man is known by patience: and his glory is to overpass unjust things.
19.12: As the roaring of a lion, so also the anger of a king: and as dew upon grass, so also his cheerfulness.
19.13: The sorrow of the father, a foolish son: and roofs continually dropping through, a woman full of brawling.
19.14: House and riches are given of the parents: but of our Lord properly a prudent wife.
19.15: Slothfulness bringeth drowsiness, and a dissolute soul shall be an hungered.
19.16: He that keepeth the commandment, keepeth his soul: but he that neglecteth his way, shall die.
19.17: He lendeth our Lord that hath mercy on the poor: and he will repay him the like.
19.18: Nurture thy son, despair not: but to the killing of him set not thy soul.
19.19: He that is impatient shall sustain damage: and when he shall take away violently, he shall add another thing.
19.20: Hear counsel, and receive discipline, that thou mayst be wise in thy later ends.
19.21: Many cogitations in the heart of a man: but the will of our Lord shall be permanent.
19.22: Aneedy man is merciful: and better is the poor than the lying man.
19.23: The fear of our Lord unto life: and in fullness he shall abide, without the visitation most noisome:
19.24: The slothful hideth his hand under the arm-hole, neither doth he put it to his mouth.
19.25: The pestilent man being whipped, the fool shall be wiser: but if thou rebuke a wiseman he will understand discipline.
19.26: He that afflicteth his father, and flyeth from his mother, is ignominious and unhappy.
19.27: Cease not, o son, to hear doctrine, neither be ignorant of the words of knowledge.
19.28: An unjust witness scorneth judgment: and the mouth of the impious devoureth iniquity.
19.29: Judgments are prepared for scorners: & hammers striking for the bodies of fools.

20.1: Wine is a luxurious thing, & drunkenness tumultuous: whosoever is delighted therewith shall not be wise.
20.2: As the roaring of a lion, so also the terror of a king: he that provoketh him, sinneth also against his own soul.
20.3: It is honour to a man that separateth himself from contentions: but all fools meddle with contumelies.
20.4: Because of cold the slothful would not plow: he shall beg therefore in the summer, and it shall not be given him.
20.5: As deep water, so counsel in the heart of a man: but a wise man shall draw it out.
20.6: Many men are called merciful: but a faithful man who shall find?
20.7: The just that walketh in his simplicity, shall leave blessed children.
20.8: The king that sitteth in the throne of judgment, dissipateth all evil with his look.
20.9: Who can say: My heart is clean, I am pure from sin?
20.10: Weight and weight, measure and measure: both are abominable before God.
20.11: By his conversation a child is perceived, if his works be clean and right.
20.12: The ear hearing, and the eye seeing, our Lord made both.
20.13: Love not sleep, lest poverty oppress thee: open thine eyes and be filled with breads.
20.14: It is naught, it is naught, saith every buyer: and when he is departed he will boast.
20.15: There is gold, and multitude of pearls: but a precious vessel the lips of knowledge.
20.16: Take his garment, that was the surety of a stranger, and for strangers take a pledge from him.
20.17: The bread of lying is sweet to a man: and afterward his mouth shall be filled with the gravel stone.
20.18: Cogitations are strengthened by counsels: and battles are to be handled by governments.
20.19: Meddle not with him that revealeth mysteries, and walketh fraudulently, and dilateth his lips.
20.20: He that curseth his father, and mother, his lamp shall be extinguished in the midst of darkness.
20.21: The inheritance whereunto haste is made in the beginning, in the later end shall lack blessing.
20.22: Say not, I will requite evil: expect our Lord, and he will deliver thee.
20.23: Weight and weight are abomination with our Lord: a deceitful balance is not good.
20.24: The steps of man are directed of our Lord: but who of men can understand his own way?
20.25: It is ruin to a man to devour saints, and afterward to retract the vows.
20.26: Awise king dissipateth the impious, and bendeth over them a triumphant arch.
20.27: The lamp of our Lord, the breath of a man, which searcheth all the secrets of the belly.
20.28: Mercy & truth keep the king, and his throne is strengthened by clemency.
20.29: The joy of youngmen their strength: and the dignity of oldmen a gray head.
20.30: The blueness of the wound shall wipe away evils: and stripes in the more secret place of the belly.

21.1: As divisions of waters, so the heart of the king is in the hand of our Lord: whither soever he will, he shall incline it.
21.2: Every way of a man seemeth to himself right: but our Lord weigheth the hearts.
21.3: To do mercy and judgment, doth more please our Lord than victims.
21.4: Exaltation of the eyes is the dilation of the heart: the lamp of the impious sin.
21.5: The cogitations of the strong are always in aboundance: but every sluggard is always in poverty.
21.6: He that gathereth treasures with a lying tongue is vain and witless, and shall stumble at the snares of death.
21.7: The robberies of the impious shall draw them down, because they would not do judgment.
21.8: The perverse way of a man is strange: but he that is clean, his work is right.
21.9: It is better to sit in a corner of the house top, than with a brawling woman, and in a common house.
21.10: The soul of the impious desireth evil, he will not have pity on his neighbour.
21.11: The pestilent man being punished, the little one will be wiser: and if he follow the wiseman, he will take knowledge.
21.12: The just deviseth concerning the house of the impious, that he may draw the impious from evil.
21.13: He that stoppeth his ear at the cry of the poor, himself also shall cry and shall not be heard.
21.14: Agift hid quencheth angers: and a gift in the bosom the greatest indignation.
21.15: It is a joy to the just to do judgment: and dread to them that work iniquity.
21.16: Aman, that shall err from the way of doctrine, shall abide in the assembly of giants.
21.17: He that loveth good cheer, shall be in poverty: he that loveth wine, and fat things, shall not be rich.
21.18: The impious shall be given for the just: and the unjust for the righteous.
21.19: It is better to dwell in a desert land, than with a brawling and angry woman.
21.20: Treasure to be desired, and oil in the habitation of the just: and the unwise man shall dissipate it.
21.21: He that followeth justice and mercy, shall find life, justice, and glory.
21.22: The wise hath scaled the city of the strong, and hath destroyed the confidence thereof.
21.23: He that keepeth his mouth, and his tongue, keepeth his soul from distresses.
21.24: The proud and arrogant is called unlearned, which in anger worketh pride.
21.25: Desires kill the slothful: for his hands would not work any thing:
21.26: all the day he longeth and desireth: but he that is just, will give, and will not cease.
21.27: The hosts of the impious abominable, because they are offered of wickedness.
21.28: Alying witness shall perish: an obedient man shall speak victory.
21.29: The impious man malapertly hardeneth his countenance: but he that is righteous, correcteth his way.
21.30: There is no wisdom, there is no prudence, there is no counsel against our Lord.
21.31: The horse is prepared to the day of battle: but our Lord giveth salvation.

22.1: Better is a good name, than much riches: above silver and gold, good grace.
22.2: The rich and poor have met one another: our Lord is the maker of both.
22.3: The subtle saw evil, and hid himself: the innocent passed by, and was afflicted with damage.
22.4: The end of modesty the fear of our Lord, riches and glory and life.
22.5: Armour and swords in the way of the perverse: but the keeper of his own soul departeth far from them.
22.6: It is a proverb: A youngman according to his way, when he is old will not depart from it.
22.7: The richman ruleth over the poor: and he that borroweth is the servant of him that lendeth.
22.8: He that soweth iniquity, shall reap evils, and with the rod of his wrath, he shall be consumed.
22.9: He that is prone to mercy shall be blessed: for of his breads he hath given to the poor. He that giveth gifts shall purchase victory and honour: but he that receiveth taketh away the soul of the giver.
22.10: Cast out the scorner, and brawling shall go forth with him, and cause shall cease and contumelies.
22.11: He that loveth cleanness of heart, for the grace of his lips, shall have the king his friend.
22.12: The eyes of our Lord keep knowledge: and the words of the just are supplanted.
22.13: The slothful saith: A lion is without, in the midst of the streets I am to be slain.
22.14: Adeep pit the mouth of a strange woman: he with whom our Lord is angry, shall fall into it.
22.15: Folly is tied together in the heart of a child, and the rod of discipline shall drive it away.
22.16: He that doth calumniate the poor to increase his riches, himself shall give to a richer, and shall be in need.
22.17: Incline thine ear, and hear the words of wisemen: and set thy heart to my doctrine:
22.18: which shall be beautiful for thee, when thou shalt keep it in thy belly, and it shall flow in thy lips.
22.19: That thy confidence may be in our Lord, wherefore I have shewed also it to thee this day.
22.20: Behold I have described it to thee three manner of ways: in cogitations and knowledge:
22.21: that I might shew thee the stability, and the words of truth, out of these to answer them that sent thee.
22.22: Do not violence to the poor, because he is poor: neither oppress the needy in the gate:
22.23: because our Lord will judge his cause, and will pierce them, that have pierced his soul.
22.24: Be not friend to an angry man, nor walk with a furious man:
22.25: lest perhaps thou learn his paths, and take scandal to thy soul.
22.26: Be not with them that stick down their hands, and that offer themselves sureties for debts:
22.27: for if thou have not wherewith to restore, what cause is there that he should take the covering from thy bed?
22.28: Transgress not the ancient bounds, which thy fathers have put.
22.29: Hast thou seen a man quick in his work? He shall stand before kings, neither shall he be before the unnoble.

23.1: When thou shalt sit to eat with a Prince, attend diligently what things are set before thy face:
23.2: and set a knife in thy throat, if notwithstanding thou have thy soul in thine own power.
23.3: Desire not his meats, in which is the breath of lying.
23.4: Labour not to be rich: but set a mean to thy prudence.
23.5: Lift not up thine eyes to the riches, which thou canst not have: because they shall make to themselves wings as of an eagle, and shall fly into heaven.
23.6: Eat not with an envious man, and desire not his meats.
23.7: Because after the similitude of a soothsayer, and diviner, he esteemeth that which he knoweth not. Eat and drink, will he say to thee: and his mind is not with thee.
23.8: The meats which thou hadst eaten thou shalt vomit up: and shalt lose thy beautiful words.
23.9: Speak not in the ears of the unwise: because they will despise the doctrine of thy speech.
23.10: Touch not the bounds of little ones: and into the field of pupils enter not.
23.11: For their near kinsman is strong: and he will judge their cause against thee.
23.12: Let thy heart enter into doctrine: and thine ears to words of knowledge.
23.13: Withdraw not discipline from a child: for if thou shalt strike him with the rod, he shall not die.
23.14: Thou shalt strike him with the rod: and deliver his soul from hell.
23.15: My son, if thy mind shall be wise, my heart shall be glad with thee:
23.16: And my reins shall rejoice, when thy lips shall speak right things.
23.17: Let not thy heart envy sinners: but in the fear of our Lord be thou all the day:
23.18: because thou shalt have hope in the later end, and thine exaltation shall not be taken away.
23.19: Hear my son, & be wise: & direct thy mind in the way.
23.20: Be not in the feasts of great drinkers, nor in their comessations, which contribute flesh together to eat:
23.21: because they that are given to drinking, and that pay shots, shall be consumed, and drowsiness shall be clothed with rags.
23.22: Hear thy father, that begot thee: and contemn not thy mother when she is old.
23.23: Buy truth, and sell not wisdom, and doctrine, and intelligence.
23.24: The father of the just rejoiceth with gladness: he that hath begotten a wiseman, shall rejoice in him.
23.25: Let thy father be glad and thy mother, and let her rejoice that bare thee.
23.26: My son give me thy heart: & let thine eyes keep my ways.
23.27: For an harlot is a deep ditch: & a strange woman a narrow pit.
23.28: She lieth in wait in the way as a robber, and whom she shall see not circumspect, she will kill.
23.29: To whom is woe? To whose father woe? To whom brawls? To whom ditches? To whom wounds without cause? To whom blood-shedding eyes?
23.30: Is it not to them that pass their time in wine, and study to drink out their cups?
23.31: Behold not wine when it waxeth yellow, when the colour thereof shall shine in the glass: it goeth in pleasantly,
23.32: but in the end, it will bite like a snake, and as a basilisk it will pour abroad poisons.
23.33: Thine eyes shall see strange women, and thy heart shall speak perverse things.
23.34: And thou shalt be as one sleeping in the midst of the sea, and as the governor fast asleep, the stern being lost.
23.35: And thou shalt say: They have beaten me, but I was not grieved: they drew me, and I felt not: When shall I awake, and find wines again?

24.1: Emulate not evil men, neither desire thou to be with them:
24.2: because their mind doth meditate robberies, and their lips speak deceits.
24.3: By wisdom the house shall be built, and by prudence it shall be strengthened.
24.4: In doctrine the cellars shall be replenished with all precious and most beautiful substance.
24.5: Awiseman is strong: and a learned man, strong and valiant.
24.6: Because war is managed by due ordering: & there shall be salvation where many counsels are.
24.7: Wisdom is high for a fool, in the gate he shall not open his mouth.
24.8: He that thinketh to do evils, shall be called a fool.
24.9: The cogitation of a fool is sin: and a detracter the abomination of men.
24.10: If thou despair being weary in the day of distress: thy strength shall be diminished.
24.11: Deliver them that are led to death: and those that are drawn to death cease not to deliver.
24.12: If thou say, I am not of force: he that seeth into the heart, he understandeth, and nothing deceiveth the keeper of thy soul, and he shall render to man according to his works.
24.13: Eat honey my son, because it is good, and the honey-comb most sweet to thy throat:
24.14: so also the doctrine of wisdom to thy soul: which when thou shalt find, thou shalt have hope in the later end, and thy hope shall not perish.
24.15: Lie not in wait, nor seek impiety in the house of the just, nor spoil his rest.
24.16: For seven times shall the just fall, and shall rise again: but the impious shall fall into evil.
24.17: When thine enemy shall fall, be not glad, and in his ruin let not thy heart rejoice:
24.18: Lest perhaps our Lord see, and it displease him, and he take away his wrath from him.
24.19: Contend not with the most wicked, nor emulate the impious:
24.20: because evil men have not hope of things to come, and the lamp of the impious shall be extinguished.
24.21: Fear our Lord, my son, and the king: & with detracters meddle not:
24.22: because their perdition shall suddenly rise; and the ruin of both who knoweth?
24.23: These things also to the wise: to know a person in judgement is not good.
24.24: They that say to the impious: Thou art just; peoples shall curse them, and tribes shall detest them.
24.25: They that rebuke him, shall be praised: and blessing shall come upon them.
24.26: He shall kiss the lips, who answereth right words.
24.27: Prepare thy work abroad, and diligently till thy ground: that afterward thou mayst build thy house.
24.28: Be not witness without cause against thy neighbour: neither allure any man with thy lips.
24.29: Say not, As he hath done to me, so will I do to him: I will render to every one according to his work.
24.30: I passed by the field of a slothful man, and by the vineyard of a foolish man:
24.31: and behold nettles had filled it wholly, and thorns had covered the face thereof, and the wall of stones was destroyed.
24.32: Which when I had seen, I laid it in my heart, and by the example I learned discipline.
24.33: Alittle I say, thou shalt sleep, a little thou shalt slumber, a little shalt thou join thy hands together to rest:
24.34: and as a post, poverty shall come to thee, & beggary as a man armed.

25.1: These also are the parables of Salomon, which the men of Ezechias king of Juda wrote out.
25.2: It is the glory of God to conceal the word, and the glory of kings to search the speech.
25.3: The heaven above, and the earth beneath, and the heart of kings is unscrutable.
25.4: Take away the rust from silver, and there shall come forth a most pure vessel:
25.5: Take away impiety from the king's countenance, and his throne shall be established with justice.
25.6: Appear not glorious before the king, and in the place of great men stand not.
25.7: For it is better that it be said to thee: Come up hither, than that thou be humbled before the Prince.
25.8: The things which thy eyes have seen, utter not quickly in a brawl: lest afterward thou canst not amend it, when thou hast dishonoured thy friend.
25.9: Treat thy cause with thy friend, and reveal not a secret to a stranger:
25.10: lest perhaps he insult against thee when he heareth, and cease not to upbraid thee. Grace and friendship deliver : which keep to thyself, lest thou become reproachful.
25.11: Apples of gold in silver beds, he that speaketh a word in his time.
25.12: Agolden earlet, and a shining precious stone, he that rebuketh a wiseman, and an obedient ear.
25.13: As the cold of snow in the day of harvest, so a faithful legate to him that sent him, maketh his soul to rest.
25.14: Clouds, and wind, and no rain following, a glorious man, and not accomplishing his promises.
25.15: By patience the Prince shall be pacified, and a soft tongue shall break hardness.
25.16: Thou hast found honey, eat that which sufficeth thee, lest perhaps being filled thou vomit it up.
25.17: Withdraw thy foot from the house of thy neighbour, lest sometime having his fill he hate thee.
25.18: Adart, and sword, and a sharp arrow, a man that speaketh false testimony against his neighbour.
25.19: Arotten tooth, and weary foot, he that hopeth upon the unfaithful in the day of distress,
25.20: and that loseth his cloak in the day of cold. Vinegar in nitre, he that singeth songs to a naughty heart. As a moth the garment, and a worm the wood: so the sadness of a man hurteth the heart.
25.21: If thine enemy shall hunger, give him meat: if he thirst, give him water to drink:
25.22: for thou shalt heap hot coals upon his head, and our Lord will reward thee.
25.23: The north-wind dissipateth rains, & a sad look the tongue that detracteth.
25.24: It is better to sit in a corner of the house top, than with a brawling woman, and in a common house.
25.25: Cold water to a thirsty soul, and good tidings from a far country.
25.26: Afountain troubled with the foot, and a vein corrupted, the just falling before the impious.
25.27: As he that eateth much honey, it is not good for him: so he that is a searcher of the majesty, shall be oppressed of the glory.
25.28: As a city being open and without compass of walls, so a man that cannot repress his spirit in speaking.

26.1: As snow in the summer, and rain in the harvest: so is glory undecent for a fool.
26.2: As a bird flying to other places, & a sparrow going whither he lift: so a curse uttered in vain shall light upon some man.
26.3: Awhip for a horse, and a snaffle for an ass, and a rod on the back of the unwise.
26.4: Answer not a fool according to his folly, lest thou be made like to him.
26.5: Answer a fool according to his folly, lest he seem to himself to be wise.
26.6: Lame of feet, and drinking iniquity, he that sendeth words by a foolish messenger.
26.7: As a lame man hath fair legs in vain: so a parable is undecent in the mouth of fools.
26.8: As he that casteth a stone into the heap of Mercury: so he that giveth honour to the unwise.
26.9: As if a thorn should grow in the hand of the drunkard: so a parable in the mouth of fools.
26.10: Judgement determineth causes: and he that putteth a fool to silence, appeaseth angers.
26.11: As a dog that returneth to his vomit, so the unwise that reiterateth his folly.
26.12: Hast thou seen a man seem to himself wise? The fool shall have hope rather than he.
26.13: The slothful saith: A lion is in the way, and a lioness in the journeys:
26.14: as a door turneth on his hinge so the slothful in his bed.
26.15: The slothful hideth his hand under the arm-hole, and is grieved if he turneth it to his mouth.
26.16: The slothful seemeth wiser to himself, than seven men speaking sentences.
26.17: As he that taketh a dog by the ears, so he that passeth by impatient, and meddleth with another man's brawl.
26.18: As he is hurtful that shooteth arrows and spears unto death:
26.19: so a man that hurteth his friend fraudulently: and when he is taken withal saith: I did it in jest.
26.20: When wood faileth, the fire shall be extinguished: and the whisperer taken away, brawls cease.
26.21: As coals to burning coals, and wood to fire, so an angry man raiseth brawls.
26.22: The words of the whisperer as it were simple, and the same come to the inmost parts of the belly.
26.23: As if thou wouldest adorn an earthen vessel with drossy silver, so swelling lips joined with a most wicked heart.
26.24: An enemy is perceived by his lips, when he shall handle deceits in his heart.
26.25: When he shall submit his voice, believe him not: because there are seven mischiefs in his heart.
26.26: He that covereth hatred fraudulently, his malice shall be revealed in the councel.
26.27: He that diggeth a pit, shall fall into it: and he that rolleth a stone, it shall return to him.
26.28: Adeceitful tongue loveth not truth, and a slipper mouth worketh ruins.

27.1: Boast not for tomorrow, being ignorant what the day to come may bring forth.
27.2: Let another praise thee, and not thine own mouth: a stranger and not thine own lips.
27.3: Astone is heavy, and sand weighty: but the anger of a fool is heavier than both.
27.4: Anger hath no mercy, nor fury breaking forth: and the violence of a moved spirit who can sustain?
27.5: Better is manifest correption, than love hidden.
27.6: Better are the wounds of him that loveth, than the fraudulent kisses of him that hateth.
27.7: Asoul that is full shall tread upon the honeycomb: and a soul that is hungry shall take bitter also for sweet.
27.8: As a bird fleeting from her nest, so a man that forsaketh his place.
27.9: The heart is delighted with ointment and diverse odours: and with the good counsels of a friend the soul is sweetened.
27.10: Thy friend, and thy father's friend do not leave: and go not into thy brother's house in the day of thine affliction. Better is a neighbour near than a brother far off.
27.11: Study wisdom my son, & make my heart joyful, that thou mayst make answer to the upbraider.
27.12: The subtle man seeing evil, hideth himself: little ones passing through have sustained evil detriments.
27.13: Take his garment that hath been surety for a stranger: and for aliens take from him a pledge.
27.14: He that blesseth his neighbour with a loud voice, rising in the night, he shall be like him that curseth.
27.15: Roofs dropping through in the day of cold, and a brawling woman are compared together:
27.16: He that retaineth her, as he that should hold the wind, and shall call it the oil of his right hand.
27.17: Iron is sharpened with iron, and a man sharpeneth the face of his friend.
27.18: He that keepeth the figtree, shall eat the fruits thereof: and he that is the keeper of his master, shall be glorified.
27.19: As in waters the countenance of them that look therein shineth, so the hearts of men are manifest to the prudent.
27.20: Hell and perdition are never filled: in like manner also the eyes of men are unsatiable.
27.21: As silver is tried in the forge, and gold in the furnace: so a man is proved by the mouth of him that praiseth. The heart of the wicked seeketh after evils, but the righteous heart seeketh after knowledge.
27.22: If thou shall bray a fool in a mortar, as when a pestle striketh upon ptisane, his folly shall not be taken from him.
27.23: Know diligently the countenance of thy cattle, and consider thy flocks:
27.24: For thou shalt not have power always: but a crown shall be given into generation and generation.
27.25: The meadows are open, and the green herbs have appeared, and the grass is gathered out of the mountains.
27.26: Lambs for thy garment: and kids the price of the field.
27.27: Let the milk of the goats suffice thee for thy meats, and for the necessities of thy house: and for victual to thy hand-maids.

28.1: The impious flyeth, no man pursuing: but the just confident as a lion, shall be without terror.
28.2: For the sins of the land the Princes thereof shall be many: & for the wisdom of a man, & the knowledge of those things that are said, the life of the Prince shall be longer.
28.3: Apoor man calumniating the poor, is like a vehement shower, whereby famine is gotten.
28.4: They that forsake the law, praise the impious: they that keep it, are set on fire against him.
28.5: Evil men think not on judgment: but they that seek after our Lord, mark all things.
28.6: Better is a poor man walking in his simplicity, than the rich in crooked ways.
28.7: He that keepeth the law is a wise son, but he that feedeth gluttons, shameth his father.
28.8: He that heapeth together riches by usuries and ocker, gathereth them for him that is liberal to the poor.
28.9: He that turneth away his ears from hearing the law, his prayer shall be execrable.
28.10: He that deceiveth the just in a wicked way, shall fall into his destruction: and the simple shall possess his goods.
28.11: The rich man seemeth to himself wise: but the poor man being prudent shall search him.
28.12: In the exultation of the just there is much glory: when the impious reign ruins of men.
28.13: He that hideth his wicked deeds, shall not be directed: but he that shall confess, and shall forsake them, shall obtain mercy.
28.14: Blessed is the man that is always fearful: but he that is of an obstinate mind, shall fall into evil.
28.15: Aroaring lion, and hungry bear, an impious Prince over the poor people.
28.16: A Prince lacking prudence, shall oppress many by calumny: but he that hateth avarice, his days shall be made long.
28.17: Aman that doth calumniate the blood of a soul, if he shall fly into a lake, no man abideth.
28.18: He that walketh simply shall be saved: he that goeth perverse ways, shall fall once.
28.19: He that tilleth his ground, shall be filled with breads: but he that pursueth idleness, shall be replenished with poverty.
28.20: Afaithful man shall be much praised: but he that hasteneth to be rich, shall not be innocent.
28.21: He that knoweth a person in judgement, doth not well: this man even for a morsel of bread forsaketh the truth.
28.22: Aman that hasteneth to be rich, and envieth others, is ignorant that poverty shall come upon him.
28.23: He that rebuketh a man, shall afterward find favour with him, more than he that by flatteries of tongue deceiveth.
28.24: He that pilfereth any thing from his father, and from his mother: & saith this is no sin, is the partaker of a mankiller.
28.25: He that exalteth and dilateth himself, raiseth brawls: but he that trusteth in our Lord, shall be healed.
28.26: He that hath confidence in his own heart, is a fool: but who so walketh wisely shall be saved.
28.27: He that giveth to the poor, shall not lack: he that despiseth him that asketh shall sustain penury.
28.28: When the impious shall rise, men shall be hid: when they shall perish, the just shall be multiplied.

29.1: The man that with stiff neck contemneth him that rebuketh, sudden destruction shall come upon him: and health shall not follow him.
29.2: In the multiplication of just men, the common people shall rejoice: when the impious shall take princedom, the people shall mourn.
29.3: Aman that loveth wisdom, maketh his father glad: but he that maintaineth harlots, shall destroy his substance.
29.4: Ajust king setteth up the land, a covetous man shall destroy it.
29.5: Aman that with fair and feigned words speaketh to his friend, spreadeth a net to his steps.
29.6: Asnare shall entangle the wicked man sinning: and the just shall praise and rejoice.
29.7: The just knoweth the cause of the poor: the impious is ignorant of knowledge.
29.8: Pestilent men dissipate a city: but the wise turn away fury.
29.9: Awise man, if he contend with a fool, whether he be angry, or whether he laugh, shall not find rest.
29.10: Men of blood hate the simple: but just men seek his soul.
29.11: Afool uttereth all his spirit: a wiseman deferreth and reserveth till afterward.
29.12: A Prince that gladly heareth words of lying, hath all his servants impious.
29.13: The poor man and the creditor have met one another: our Lord is illuminator of both.
29.14: The king that judgeth the poor in truth, his throne shall be replenished for ever.
29.15: Rod and rebuke giveth wisdom: but the child that is left to his own will, confoundeth his mother.
29.16: In the multiplication of the impious, wickedness shall be multiplied, and the just shall see the ruins of them.
29.17: Nurture thy son, and he shall refresh thee, and shall give delights to thy soul.
29.18: When prophecy shall fail, the people shall be dissipated: but he that keepeth the Law, is blessed.
